checks.a_analysis: The output JSON accurately reflects the text found in the source document. The title 'Building Footprint Update Status' is directly present at the top of the page. The image alt text is descriptive of the visual elements and the text contained within the graphics (e.g., map title, legend details), which is an expected behavior for image processing in semantic conversion.
There is no contact information (phone numbers, emails, URLs) present in the source document or the output JSON.

The source document consists of a single page/slide. This page is entirely accounted for in the output JSON, with the text title and the three main graphical elements (logos and the map infographic) represented.
The source document does not contain any tables.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The output JSON captures the primary text heading of the document. The text embedded within the map graphic (title, legend, scale) is successfully preserved and conveyed via the AI-generated alt text for the second image, ensuring the information is not lost during the conversion process.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The heading text in the output matches the source document exactly. No wording changes or meaning-altering differences were observed.

checks.a_analysis: The text 'Building Footprint Update Status' appears as the title in the source document. In the output JSON, it is correctly represented as both the metadata title and the primary heading for the section. No other text content or images are duplicated in the output.

The output document contains a single top-level heading ('Building Footprint Update Status') which correctly reflects the main title of the source document. There are no subheadings or lower-level headings present in either the source or the output, resulting in a flat but correct hierarchy.

The source document does not contain any tables. The graphical information presented in the legend is part of the main image and was correctly excluded from table conversion. Therefore, no table structure corruption occurred.

The reading order in the output follows a logical top-to-bottom sequence: Title -> Logo -> Main Map Image -> Footer Logo. This flow faithfully represents the intended organizational structure of the original document.

The source document does not contain any formal text-based lists. The visual list presented in the map's legend is appropriately captured within the alternative text of the map image rather than being converted into a structural list element. There are no incorrect list conversions.
No links are present in the source document, and no links have been added or injected into the output document.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output reorganizes the page elements from the PDF. In the PDF, branding logos (EMAP, NC Emergency Management) are located in the bottom corners of the page. In the HTML, these logos are moved to the top of the content area, appearing after the document title and before the map. While this is a change in visual organization, it does not confuse the meaning of the document and follows a logical reading order for a web-based layout. The primary information (the map) remains central and clearly legible. Other differences like font and margins are expected in an HTML conversion.
There are no formal HTML tables in the document. The legend information is part of the map graphic, and no tabular data was misrendered as plain text or otherwise corrupted.

The 'DPS Emergency Management NC DEPARTMENT OF PUBLIC SAFETY' branding logo located in the top-left header of the source PDF is missing from the HTML output. While a much smaller version of the logo exists within the map frame, the primary agency branding from the document header is absent in the converted version. This is a meaningful branding element that identifies the source of the document.
checks.b_evidence_source: DPS logo and 'Emergency Management NC DEPARTMENT OF PUBLIC SAFETY' text in the blue header at the top left of the PDF page.
checks.b_evidence_output: The top of the HTML starts with the text title 'Building Footprint Update Status' followed by other logos; the specific DPS header branding is missing.

checks.a_analysis: The North Carolina Emergency Management triangle logo is duplicated in the HTML output. In the source PDF, this standalone logo appears once in the bottom-right corner (in addition to a version inside the map graphic). In the HTML screenshot, the standalone logo is rendered twice: once as a large image at the top of the page (below the EMAP logo) and again at the bottom-right corner of the page. This is a visual duplication of body content.
checks.b_evidence_source: NC Emergency Management logo appearing as a standalone element at the bottom right of the page.
checks.b_evidence_output: The NC Emergency Management triangle logo appears once as a large image at the top of the document (below the EMAP logo) and again at the bottom right of the page.
checks.b_evidence_location: Top of the document and bottom right corner of the document
